topic: Quantum computing applications in cryptography post-2022
final_review:
The advent of quantum computing has significant implications for cryptography, as it can potentially break certain classical encryption algorithms (Jiang et al., 2021; Preskill, 2018). In the post-2022 era, researchers have been exploring the applications of quantum computing in cryptography, with a focus on developing quantum-resistant cryptographic schemes (Fernández‐Caramés and Fraga‐Lamas, 2020; Alagic et al., 2022). One of the key areas of research is the development of lattice-based security and authentication schemes for smart-grid networks (Shekhawat and Gupta, 2024). These schemes are considered to be quantum-resistant, meaning they can withstand attacks from quantum computers (Lenstra and Verheul, 2001).

Another area of research is the development of secure key exchange protocols, which enable secure communication between two parties (Juels and Wattenberg, 1999). The use of blockchain technology is also being explored in the context of quantum computing and cryptography (Fernández‐Caramés and Fraga‐Lamas, 2020; Yaga et al., 2018). Blockchain technology can provide a secure and transparent way to conduct transactions, and it has the potential to be used in a variety of applications, including supply chain management and voting systems (Yaga et al., 2018). However, the use of blockchain technology in the post-quantum era is still in its early stages, and more research is needed to fully understand its potential (Fernández‐Caramés, 2019).

In terms of the potential risks and challenges associated with quantum computing and cryptography, researchers have identified several areas of concern (Vaishnavi and Pillai, 2021). One of the key risks is the potential for quantum computers to break certain classical encryption algorithms, which could compromise the security of sensitive information (Preskill, 2018). Another area of concern is the potential for quantum computers to be used in cyber attacks, which could have significant consequences for individuals and organizations (Vaishnavi and Pillai, 2021).

Despite the potential risks and challenges, researchers are also exploring the potential benefits of quantum computing and cryptography (Alagic et al., 2022). One of the key benefits is the potential for quantum computers to provide enhanced security, which could enable secure communication over long distances (Preskill, 2018). Another area of benefit is the potential for quantum computers to be used in simulations and modeling, which could have significant implications for fields such as medicine and finance (Preskill, 2018).

In conclusion, the applications of quantum computing in cryptography post-2022 are a rapidly evolving field, with significant potential benefits and risks (Alagic et al., 2022). Researchers are exploring a variety of areas, including lattice-based security and authentication schemes, secure key exchange protocols, and the use of blockchain technology (Fernández‐Caramés and Fraga‐Lamas, 2020; Shekhawat and Gupta, 2024). However, more research is needed to fully understand the potential of quantum computing and cryptography, and to address the potential risks and challenges associated with these technologies (Vaishnavi and Pillai, 2021).

References:
Alagic, G., Apon, D., and Cooper, D. A. (2022). Status report on the third round of the NIST Post-Quantum Cryptography Standardization process. https://doi.org/10.6028/nist.ir.8413
Fernández‐Caramés, T. M., and Fraga‐Lamas, P. (2020). Towards Post-Quantum Blockchain: A Review on Blockchain Cryptography Resistant to Quantum Computing Attacks. https://doi.org/10.1109/access.2020.2968985
Fernández‐Caramés, T. M. (2019). From Pre-Quantum to Post-Quantum IoT Security: A Survey on Quantum-Resistant Cryptosystems for the Internet of Things. https://doi.org/10.1109/jiot.2019.2958788
Jiang, W., Han, B., and Habibi, M. A. (2021). The Road Towards 6G: A Comprehensive Survey. https://doi.org/10.1109/ojcoms.2021.3057679
Juels, A., and Wattenberg, M. (1999). A fuzzy commitment scheme. https://doi.org/10.1145/319709.319714
Lenstra, A. K., and Verheul, E. R. (2001). Selecting Cryptographic Key Sizes. https://doi.org/10.1007/s00145-001-0009-4
Preskill, J. (2018). Quantum Computing in the NISQ era and beyond. https://doi.org/10.22331/q-2018-08-06-79
Shekhawat, H., and Gupta, D. (2024). A survey on lattice‐based security and authentication schemes for smart‐grid networks in the post‐quantum era. https://doi.org/10.1002/cpe.8080
Vaishnavi, A., and Pillai, S. (2021). Cybersecurity in the Quantum Era-A Study of Perceived Risks in Conventional Cryptography and Discussion on Post Quantum Methods. https://doi.org/10.1088/1742-6596/1964/4/042002
Yaga, D., Mell, P., and Roby, N. (2018). Blockchain technology overview. https://doi.org/10.6028/nist.ir.8202
